meta.title: A Day in the Life of a CCS Student
meta.description: Navigate the pressures of a Computer Science curriculum at AUF.

start.text: Dodo is rubbing his eyes while Cassie clicks rapidly in Figma. "D'yan na 'to pantay, Cass. If we use this grid, may 8-pixel gap sa dulo. It looks... messy. Pixel gore levels of messy."
start.speaker: Dodo

figma_crisis.text: Cassie sighs, "I know! We need the buttons to be perfectly square, zero leftover space in this 1920x1080 frame. Minimalist aesthetic nga 'di ba?"
figma_crisis.speaker: Cassie

gcd_logic.text: Dodo looks at the screen. "So we need the largest number that divides both 1920 and 1080? A Greatest Common Divisor? How do we find it fast? My brain is at 2% battery."
gcd_logic.speaker: Dodo

scene1_complete_euclid.text: Using remainders is blazing fast! The calculation works instantly, and the buttons fit perfectly into the 1920x1080 grid. "Pixel perfect!" Cassie exclaims. But wait, you hear a frantic knocking on the door. It's Inigo.
scene1_complete_euclid.speaker: Narrator

scene1_suboptimal.text: Dodo watches the screen compute. "Hmm, checking every integer downwards or listing prime factor trees works, but this is taking longer than expected. I wonder if there's a faster way to do this using remainders... Still, we got the buttons to fit perfectly!" Cassie cheers. But wait, you hear a frantic knocking on the door. It's Inigo.
scene1_suboptimal.speaker: Narrator

morning_panic.text: Inigo has one sock on and looks terrified. "I'm dead! I have to feed the cat, wash dishes, pack my bag, and fix the bed in 15 minutes! Pero 'di ko alam anong uunahin!"
morning_panic.speaker: Inigo

permutation_logic.text: Mikaela leans against the doorframe. "Chill, Inigo. You're frozen by decision paralysis. Do you even realize how many possible sequences you're trying to simulate?"
permutation_logic.speaker: Mikaela

end_chapter.text:
Inigo finally picks a path and starts moving. 24 permutations down to one successful morning. "Recursive thinking saved my life!" he shouts while running out.

*Mathematical Foundations Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: Cassie is staring intently at her laptop screen in VS Code. "Guys, our database login system keeps accepting duplicate user registrations. It's messing up the backend!"
start.speaker: Cassie

unique_suboptimal.text: Cassie runs the nested loop. "Hmm, comparing every registration against every other user is taking longer than expected. My CPU usage is spiking. I wonder if there's a faster way to do this with a Set or frequency tracker... But hey, no duplicates!" Just then, Mikaela bursts into the room. "The Alco quiz results are out! Whoever got the highest score has to treat the class!"
unique_suboptimal.speaker: Cassie

choices.label: Sort entire array to find max

unique_elements_node.text: "Good call! By checking if all usernames in the collection are distinct, we prevented duplicates," Cassie smiles. Just then, Mikaela bursts into the room holding up her phone. "The Alco quiz results are out! Whoever got the highest score has to treat the whole class to lunch!"
unique_elements_node.speaker: Mikaela

choices.label: Sort entire array to find max

max_suboptimal.text: Dodo watches the sorting algorithm execute. "Hmm, sorting the entire array just to find one top score is taking longer than expected. I wonder if there's a faster way by just iterating once O(N)... Wow, I got the highest score... goodbye allowance. Come on, let's head out. We need to catch a jeep at the terminal."
max_suboptimal.speaker: Dodo

max_element_node.text: "By iterating through the array and maintaining a variable tracking the largest value, we found the top scorer!" Dodo groans, "Wow, I got the highest score... goodbye allowance. Come on, let's head out. We need to catch a jeep at the terminal."
max_element_node.speaker: Dodo

sequential_search_node.text: At the bustling jeepney terminal, you scan linearly from start to finish to find the correct jeep route. You board successfully and arrive at the karaoke spot! Inigo is flipping through a massive, sorted karaoke songbook looking for his favorite OPM song. "There are thousands of songs here! How do we find it fast?"
sequential_search_node.speaker: Inigo

suboptimal_karaoke_node.text: Inigo flips through the massive songbook page by page. "Hmm, this linear scan is taking way longer than expected! I wonder if there's a faster way to do this by halving the book... Finally found it!" Before he sings, Dodo pulls out some index cards. "Guys, I need to organize my flashcards for our upcoming ethics enumeration quiz. How do I insert new cards into my sorted stack?"
suboptimal_karaoke_node.speaker: Inigo

binary_search_node.text: By repeatedly dividing the sorted songbook interval in half O(log N), Inigo finds his OPM song efficiently! Before he sings, Dodo pulls out some index cards. "Guys, I need to organize my flashcards for our upcoming ethics enumeration quiz. How do I insert new cards into my sorted stack?"
binary_search_node.speaker: Cassie

interpolation_search_node.text: Knowing the songbook is uniformly distributed alphabetically, you estimate the target position based on value. Instant match! Before Inigo sings, Dodo pulls out some index cards. "Guys, I need to organize my flashcards for our upcoming ethics enumeration quiz. How do I insert new cards into my sorted stack?"
interpolation_search_node.speaker: Cassie

insertion_suboptimal.text: Dodo stuffs the flashcards in randomly. "Hmm, inserting cards without locating the correct position is taking longer than expected when I try to find them later! I wonder if there's an insertion search method to maintain order... Stack sorted!" Cassie then pulls out her phone to play an RPG. "I'm trying to add my friend's username in the guild search, but I only know part of their handle."
insertion_suboptimal.speaker: Dodo

insertion_search_node.text: By locating the correct position for each element within the sorted sub-list, Dodo keeps his ethics flashcards perfectly ordered! Cassie then pulls out her phone to play a multiplayer RPG game. "I'm trying to add my friend's username in the guild search, but I only know part of their handle."
insertion_search_node.speaker: Narrator

bf_string_node.text: Aligning the pattern at every possible position until a match is found, Cassie successfully locates and adds her friend to the RPG guild party!
bf_string_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From debugging login validation to finding jeepneys and belting out OPM hits, your searching and pattern matching skills saved the day!

*Searching & Pattern Matching Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: Dodo is staring at Figma, groaning. "Guys, my Figma asset panel is a complete disaster. I have all these cluttered UI icons, and I need to organize them by file size before I export. But doing it one by one feels like torture!"
start.speaker: Dodo

selection_sort_node.text: Dodo watches the icons swap. "Hmm, comparing and selecting them one by one O(N^2) is taking longer than expected, but my Figma assets are finally organized perfectly!" Cassie then calls everyone over. "Hey, I'm compiling a massive list of user data for our student management system. Before we can test the search functions, we need to sort thousands of entries by ID!"
selection_sort_node.speaker: Cassie

bubble_sort_node.text: Cassie tries stepping through the list, comparing adjacent elements and swapping them. "Ugh, O(N^2) is taking way too long for thousands of entries! My laptop fans are screaming!" Mikaela chimes in, "We need a stable divide-and-conquer approach to split the list into halves, sort them recursively, and merge them back together!"
bubble_sort_node.speaker: Mikaela

merge_sort_node.text: The merge sort compiles the list flawlessly and maintains stable ID ordering. Inigo looks at the code. "That was smooth! But what if we want an in-place efficient partition method? Picking a pivot to divide the array into smaller and larger elements?"
merge_sort_node.speaker: Inigo

quicksort_node.text: Quicksort partitions the array around the pivot and sorts the management system entries at blazing O(N log N) speed! Dodo rubs his chin, "What if memory space is strictly limited and we need a guaranteed priority queue approach? Like building a binary heap and extracting the max/min elements?"
quicksort_node.speaker: Dodo

heapsort_node.text: Building a binary heap allows you to extract elements in perfect priority order without using extra memory space! The student management system is now fully optimized and ready for deployment.
heapsort_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From organizing Figma UI icons to sorting massive user databases with divide-and-conquer and heaps, your sorting algorithm mastery is unmatched!

*Sorting Algorithms Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: Inigo bursts into the lounge, panting. "Guys, I woke up late and need to get to the Computer Science building! I'm looking at a map of the campus walkways, noting that some paths are crowded with students (high weight) while others are clear (low weight). How do I find the absolute shortest path?"
start.speaker: Inigo

dijkstra_suboptimal.text: Inigo dashes down a random path and gets stuck behind a massive crowd of freshmen. "Hmm, taking a random shortcut without calculating edge weights is taking way longer than expected! I wonder if there's a greedy shortest-path algorithm to calculate this... Finally made it to the lounge!"
dijkstra_suboptimal.speaker: Inigo

dijkstra_node.text: By finding the shortest path from the starting node to all other nodes in the weighted graph with non-negative edge weights, Inigo arrives instantly! At the student lounge, Dodo and Cassie are untangling cables.
dijkstra_node.speaker: Narrator

lounge_node.text: "Help us connect several workstations in the lab using the minimum amount of wiring possible to save on costs, ensuring every desk is linked to the network without unnecessary loops!" Dodo explains.
lounge_node.speaker: Dodo

mst_suboptimal.text: Dodo starts plugging cables everywhere. "Hmm, creating a complete mesh network is taking longer than expected and wasting so much expensive wiring! I wonder if there's a Minimum Spanning Tree algorithm to do this greedily... Still, the network is up!" Cassie then opens her laptop to play an RPG game.
mst_suboptimal.speaker: Dodo

prims_node.text: By starting from a single node and greedily adding the cheapest edge to a new vertex, you build a flawless Minimum Spanning Tree! Cassie then opens her laptop to play an RPG game.
prims_node.speaker: Narrator

kruskals_node.text: By sorting all edges by weight and adding the cheapest ones that do not form a cycle, you build a cost-effective Minimum Spanning Tree! Cassie then opens her laptop to play an RPG game.
kruskals_node.speaker: Narrator

rpg_tree_node.text: Cassie points to her screen. "I'm trying to unlock a specific node in this character skill tree in my RPG game. How should I explore the graph?"
rpg_tree_node.speaker: Cassie

tree_suboptimal.text: Cassie randomly clicks skill nodes. "Hmm, clicking without a traversal strategy is taking longer than expected and wasting my skill points. I wonder if there's a systematic BFS or DFS way to explore this... Finally got the ultimate skill!"
tree_suboptimal.speaker: Cassie

dfs_node.text: Exploring the graph by visiting a node and recursively visiting all nodes along a single branch as deep as possible before backtracking unlocks the skill perfectly!
dfs_node.speaker: Narrator

bfs_node.text: Exploring the graph layer by layer, visiting all neighbors of a node before moving to the next level of depth unlocks the skill flawlessly!
bfs_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From navigating campus walkways to building minimum spanning networks and mastering RPG skill trees, your graph and network algorithms are legendary!

*Graph & Network Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: In the basement of the engineering wing, Dodo is standing in front of a vintage vending machine. "I’ve just finished a project and have a handful of different coins. I need to provide the exact amount for a bag of chips using the smallest number of coins possible. How do we calculate the optimal coin breakdown?"
start.speaker: Dodo

change_making_suboptimal.text: Dodo starts stuffing random coins into the slot. "Hmm, trying combinations blindly without dynamic programming is taking longer than expected, and the machine keeps spitting them back out! I wonder if there's an optimization algorithm to find the minimum coins... Finally got the chips!"
change_making_suboptimal.speaker: Dodo

change_making_node.text: By finding the minimum number of coins of specific denominations needed to make the target total using dynamic programming, Dodo gets his chips instantly!
change_making_node.speaker: Narrator

bench_node.text: While sitting on a long bench nearby eating chips, Cassie notices someone dropped a line of coins. "Look! But there's a security laser sensor that prevents us from picking up any two coins that are directly adjacent. How can we get the maximum total value?"
bench_node.speaker: Cassie

coin_row_suboptimal.text: Cassie grabs a single 10-peso coin and the laser alarm beeps! "Hmm, grabbing greedily without checking non-adjacent subproblems is yielding way less money than expected! I wonder if there's a dynamic programming recurrence for this... At least we got 10 pesos!"
coin_row_suboptimal.speaker: Cassie

coin_row_node.text: By solving the dynamic programming recurrence to find the maximum value of coins picked from a row without adjacent selections, you collect the ultimate bounty!
coin_row_node.speaker: Narrator

mobile_game_node.text: Later in the student lounge, Mikaela is playing a quick grid-based mobile game. "You must move a character from the top-left corner of a room to the bottom-right, grabbing as much loot as possible while moving only right or down. What's the optimal path?"
mobile_game_node.speaker: Mikaela

coin_collecting_suboptimal.text: Mikaela moves her character zigzag across the room. "Hmm, wandering without calculating grid subproblems is taking longer than expected and missing the best loot. I wonder if there's a dynamic programming table approach for this... Game over, but good run!"
coin_collecting_suboptimal.speaker: Mikaela

coin_collecting_node.text: By building a dynamic programming grid table to collect the maximum coins while moving right or down, Mikaela sets a new high score!
coin_collecting_node.speaker: Narrator

huffman_node.text: It’s 11:00 PM and Inigo is stressing out. "I am finalizing our massive capstone documentation file, but it needs to be compressed to fit the submission portal's strict 5MB limit! How can we compress it without losing data?"
huffman_node.speaker: Inigo

huffman_suboptimal.text: Inigo starts deleting important paragraphs. "Hmm, deleting text blindly is compromising our documentation quality and taking longer to re-edit! I wonder if there's a lossless greedy compression algorithm to assign shorter binary codes to frequent characters... Finally zipped it!"
huffman_suboptimal.speaker: Inigo

huffman_coding_node.text: Using a greedy algorithm for lossless data compression that assigns shorter binary codes to more frequent characters, the file compresses to 4.8MB perfectly!
huffman_coding_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From vending machines and bench bounties to grid game high scores and flawless file compression, your dynamic programming and optimization skills are elite!

*Dynamic Programming & Optimization Chapter Completed.*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

start.text: It is the night before graduation, and Dodo is panicking in the computer lab. "I am debugging a complex logic circuit for our final demonstration! I find myself trying a specific configuration, hitting a dead end where the logic fails. How should I navigate this?"
start.speaker: Dodo

backtracking_suboptimal.text: Dodo keeps adding wires blindly to the failing circuit. "Hmm, pushing forward without stepping back to the last working state is causing a massive short circuit and taking longer than expected! I wonder if there's a systematic backtracking method to undo invalid partial solutions... Finally fixed the board!"
backtracking_suboptimal.speaker: Dodo

backtracking_node.text: Using a systematic trial-and-error method that builds a solution incrementally and backtracks as soon as a partial solution violates constraints, Dodo isolates the working circuit perfectly!
backtracking_node.speaker: Narrator

pouch_node.text: Now at midnight, Cassie is trying to pack her essentials into a small tech pouch for tomorrow’s presentation. "I have so many chargers, adapters, and cables, but the pouch has a strict weight limit! How do I find the best combination without trying every single one?"
pouch_node.speaker: Cassie

pouch_suboptimal.text: Cassie tries stuffing every single adapter combination into the pouch one by one. "Hmm, trying combinations exhaustively without bounding is taking way longer than expected, and my pouch zipper almost broke! I wonder if there's a Branch and Bound technique to prune branches that exceed the weight limit early... Finally packed!"
pouch_suboptimal.speaker: Cassie

branch_bound_node.text: Using an optimization technique that explores a state-space tree but uses bounds to prune branches that cannot yield a better solution than the current best, Cassie packs the optimal tech pouch effortlessly!
branch_bound_node.speaker: Narrator

end_chapter.text:
From backtracking logic circuits to bounding tech pouch combinations, your state-space search mastery has earned you your degree! The AUF journey is complete.

*State-Space Search Chapter Completed. You are the ultimate CCS student!*
end_chapter.speaker: Narrator

algorithms.title: EUCLID'S ALGORITHM
algorithms.definition: An efficient method for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two numbers by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of its division by the smaller number.
algorithms.useCase: Used in Cryptography (RSA), simplifying fractions, and optimizing UI grid layouts.

algorithms.title: CONSECUTIVE INTEGER CHECK
algorithms.definition: A brute-force method to find the GCD by checking all integers starting from the minimum of the two numbers downwards until a common divisor is found.
algorithms.useCase: Simple implementations where efficiency is not critical or for educational purposes.

algorithms.title: MIDDLE SCHOOL PROCEDURE
algorithms.definition: Finding the GCD by identifying the prime factorization of both numbers and multiplying the common prime factors together.
algorithms.useCase: Manual calculations and teaching the fundamental theorem of arithmetic.

algorithms.title: FACTORIAL (RECURSION)
algorithms.definition: Calculating the product of all positive integers up to n using a recursive function where f(n) = n * f(n-1).
algorithms.useCase: Calculating permutations, combinations, and understanding recursion.

algorithms.title: SEQUENTIAL SEARCH
algorithms.definition: Scans a list linearly from start to finish to find a target value or determine its absence.
algorithms.useCase: Finding the correct jeep to take in a jeepney terminal.

algorithms.title: BINARY SEARCH
algorithms.definition: Efficiently finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
algorithms.useCase: Finding your favorite OPM song in a karaoke songbook.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function binarySearch(arr, target):
  low = 0, high = arr.length - 1
  while low <= high:
    mid = (low + high) / 2
    if arr[mid] == target: return mid
    else if arr[mid] < target: low = mid + 1
    else: high = mid - 1
  return -1

algorithms.title: INTERPOLATION SEARCH
algorithms.definition: An improvement on binary search for uniformly distributed sorted data that estimates the target's position based on its value.
algorithms.useCase: Finding your favorite OPM song in a karaoke songbook.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function interpolationSearch(arr, target):
  low = 0, high = arr.length - 1
  while low <= high and target >= arr[low] and target <= arr[high]:
    pos = low + ((target - arr[low]) * (high - low)) / (arr[high] - arr[low])
    if arr[pos] == target: return pos
    else if arr[pos] < target: low = pos + 1
    else: high = pos - 1
  return -1

algorithms.title: INSERTION SEARCH
algorithms.definition: Locates the correct position for an element within a sorted sub-list to maintain order.
algorithms.useCase: Organizing your flashcards for your upcoming ethics enumeration quiz.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function insertionSearch(arr, key):
  i = arr.length - 1
  while i >= 0 and arr[i] > key:
    arr[i + 1] = arr[i]
    i--
  arr[i + 1] = key
  return i + 1

algorithms.title: MAX ELEMENT
algorithms.definition: Iterates through an array while maintaining a variable that tracks the largest value encountered.
algorithms.useCase: Finding who got the highest score in the alco quiz who will treat the whole class.

algorithms.title: UNIQUE ELEMENTS
algorithms.definition: Checks if all elements in a collection are distinct by comparing each pair or using a frequency tracker.
algorithms.useCase: Used in unique username login systems for the database system in VS code.

algorithms.title: BF STRING MATCHING
algorithms.definition: Checks for a pattern within a text by aligning it at every possible position until a match is found.
algorithms.useCase: Finding your friend to add him in the rpg game (Brute Force).
algorithms.pseudocode:
function bfStringMatch(text, pattern):
  n = text.length, m = pattern.length
  for i = 0 to n - m:
    j = 0
    while j < m and text[i + j] == pattern[j]: j++
    if j == m: return i
  return -1

algorithms.title: SELECTION SORT
algorithms.definition: Repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion of a list and swaps it into its correct position.
algorithms.useCase: Drag and dropping unorganized and cluttered UI icons for your figma project by file size one by one.

algorithms.title: BUBBLE SORT
algorithms.definition: Repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID.

algorithms.title: MERGE SORT
algorithms.definition: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits a list into halves, sorts them recursively, and merges the sorted halves back together.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID stably.

algorithms.title: QUICKSORT
algorithms.definition: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that picks a "pivot" and partitions the array into elements smaller and larger than the pivot.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID efficiently.

algorithms.title: HEAPSORT
algorithms.definition: A comparison-based sorting technique that builds a Binary Heap and repeatedly extracts the maximum/minimum element.
algorithms.useCase: Compiling a massive list of user data for a management system. Sorting thousands of entries by ID with priority heap.

algorithms.title: DFS (DEPTH-FIRST SEARCH)
algorithms.definition: Explores a graph by visiting a node and then recursively visiting all nodes along a single branch as deep as possible before backtracking.
algorithms.useCase: Unlocking a specific node in a character skill tree in an rpg game.

algorithms.title: BFS (BREADTH-FIRST SEARCH)
algorithms.definition: Explores a graph layer by layer, visiting all neighbors of a node before moving to the next level of depth.
algorithms.useCase: Unlocking a specific node in a character skill tree in an rpg game.

algorithms.title: PRIM'S ALGORITHM
algorithms.definition: A greedy algorithm that builds a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) by starting from a single node and adding the cheapest edge to a new vertex.
algorithms.useCase: Connecting several lab workstations using the minimum amount of wiring possible to save on costs without loops.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function primMST(graph):
  mst = [], visited = new Set([0])
  pq = new PriorityQueue()
  pq.pushAll(graph.edgesFrom(0))
  while visited.size < graph.numVertices:
    edge = pq.popCheapest()
    if !visited.has(edge.to):
      visited.add(edge.to)
      mst.push(edge)
      pq.pushAll(graph.edgesFrom(edge.to))
  return mst

algorithms.title: KRUSKAL'S ALGORITHM
algorithms.definition: A greedy algorithm that builds an MST by sorting all edges by weight and adding the cheapest ones that do not form a cycle.
algorithms.useCase: Connecting several lab workstations using the minimum amount of wiring possible to save on costs without loops.

algorithms.title: DIJKSTRA'S SHORTEST PATH
algorithms.definition: Finds the shortest path from a starting node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights.
algorithms.useCase: Finding the quickest campus walkway path to get to the Computer Science building when waking up late.

algorithms.title: COIN ROW
algorithms.definition: A dynamic programming problem to find the maximum value of coins picked from a row, provided no two adjacent coins are chosen.
algorithms.useCase: Collecting dropped coins along a long bench protected by security laser sensors preventing adjacent pickups.
algorithms.pseudocode:
function coinRow(coins):
  n = coins.length
  if n == 0: return 0
  if n == 1: return coins[0]
  dp = new Array(n + 1)
  dp[0] = 0, dp[1] = coins[0]
  for i = 2 to n:
    dp[i] = max(coins[i-1] + dp[i-2], dp[i-1])
  return dp[n]

algorithms.title: CHANGE MAKING
algorithms.definition: Finds the minimum number of coins (of specific denominations) needed to make a target total.
algorithms.useCase: Providing exact change for a bag of chips at a vintage vending machine using minimal coins.

algorithms.title: COIN COLLECTING (GRID)
algorithms.definition: A dynamic programming task to find the path in a grid that collects the maximum number of coins while moving only right or down.
algorithms.useCase: Moving a character from one corner of a room to the other in a grid-based mobile game grabbing maximum loot.

algorithms.title: HUFFMAN CODING
algorithms.definition: A greedy algorithm used for lossless data compression that assigns shorter binary codes to more frequent characters.
algorithms.useCase: Compressing a massive documentation file at 11:00 PM to fit a portal's 5MB limit.

algorithms.title: BACKTRACKING
algorithms.definition: A systematic trial-and-error method that builds a solution incrementally and backtracks as soon as a partial solution violates constraints.
algorithms.useCase: Debugging a complex logic circuit; stepping back to the last working state when hitting a dead end.

algorithms.title: BRANCH AND BOUND
algorithms.definition: An optimization technique that explores a state-space tree but uses bounds to prune branches that cannot yield a better solution than the current best.
algorithms.useCase: Packing tech pouch essentials for tomorrow's presentation, discarding heavy cable combinations early.
